content.assessment: Grade-splitting addresses transport and access constraints for the most transport-vulnerable cohort (younger children) by keeping them local, while shifting the capacity and specialist-teacher problem to a consolidated upper-grade site where economies of scale are more attainable. This is coherent with a network planning logic that recognizes uneven vulnerability to travel burden by age. However, site selection for the consolidated tier risks reproducing the same inter-municipal pull dynamics already documented in Hungarian micro-regions, and transport infrastructure gaps in rural regions could undermine the very commute this model imposes on older children.
opportunities: Route-based, multi-village transport logistics models (as demonstrated in a minority-language school bus program) offer a transferable operational template for moving upper-grade pupils, though domestic applicability is inferred rather than confirmed (F-live-school_network_planning-08)
strengths: Reduces commute burden specifically for younger pupils by preserving local lower-grade access
Concentrates upper-grade capacity where specialist staffing and class-size thresholds are more feasible to meet at scale
threats: Selection of the consolidated upper-grade site could trigger the same informal inter-municipal competition for pupils documented elsewhere, distorting a purely geographic-efficiency rationale for site choice (F-live-school_network_planning-09)

Underlying population decline continues to shrink the pool of upper-grade pupils feeding the consolidated site, potentially undermining the capacity gains the model seeks (F-live-school_network_planning-01)
weaknesses: The model still requires functioning transport for upper-grade pupils, which is precisely where documented public transport frequency and scheduling gaps are most acute in North-Eastern, South-Western, and Western rural regions (F-live-school_network_planning-07)
